---
title: "Data Science & Digital Skills Programme"
subtitle: "Building core competencies for modern, reproducible research"

about:
  id: hero-heading
  template: jolla
  image: assets/Logo_white_500.png
  links:
    - icon: twitter
      text: Twitter
      href: https://twitter.com
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com
format:
  html:
    theme:
      - default
      - ./uon.scss
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    page-layout: full
    sidebar: false
---

:::{#hero-heading}
The **Data Science & Digital Skills Programme (DS&DSP)** is a one-year, in-person training initiative that equips researchers with the essential digital competencies for modern, reproducible, and data-intensive research. 
:::


::: {.callout-quote}
*"Modern research projects routinely involve code, large data sets and complex computational workflows.  
Yet many researchers have limited exposure to software engineering, digital infrastructure or best practices for managing data.  The DS&DSP addresses this gap by providing a structured training pathway aligned with open science and research integrity principles."*
:::

::: {.two-col-grid}
::: {.item}

<br>

## Introduction

The Data Science & Digital Skills Programme (DS&DSP) is a one-year, in-person training initiative that equips researchers with the essential digital competencies for modern, reproducible, and data-intensive research. This curriculum is built around clear navigation and intuitive organisation so that participants can easily locate resources and modules on the website.  All materials are mobile‑friendly and accessible, ensuring that learners can engage with content on different devices.  
:::

::: {.item}

<br>

![](assets/learning_diagram.png)
:::
:::

::: {.dark}

<h2> The 5 principles of the DS&DSP programme </h2>

::: {.five-col-grid}

::: {.item}
![](assets/foundation.png){.round-img width="120px"}
**Structured progression**  
<br>
The programme is organised into foundation, intermediate and advanced tiers, allowing participants to build their skills incrementally and revisit concepts as needed.

:::

::: {.item}
![](assets/hands-on.png){.round-img width="120px"}
**Hands-on learning**  
<br>
Each session combines didactic lectures and collaborative exercises such as scenario-based tasks to reinforce understanding through practice.

:::

::: {.item}
![](assets/open-science.png){.round-img width="120px"}
**Open science focus**  
<br>
Modules emphasise FAIR (findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable) data principles, reproducible workflows and responsible data governance.

:::

::: {.item}
![](assets/discipline.png){.round-img width="120px"}
**Discipline-agnostic**  
<br>
Although developed for BBSRC doctoral researchers, the methods and tools apply across research domains, and later tiers offer optional domain-specific modules.

:::

::: {.item}
![](assets/portfolio.png){.round-img width="120px"}
**Portfolio-building**  
<br>
Participants develop a digital portfolio of skills, improve their competencies and gain tools that they can be reused in their own research.

:::
:::
:::

---

### Structure

The DS&DSP is structured to support learners

[label](https://dev.azure.com/UniversityOfNottingham/DRS/_library)

::: {.two-col-grid}
::: {.item}

#### Training tiers
#### Training tiers

::: callout-EDS
##### Foundation 
Essential Digital Skills (*year 1; mandatory*).  Four half‑day sessions introduce the core tools, policies and principles required for computational research.
:::

<br>

::: callout-MDS
##### Intermediate
 Methods for Data Science (*year 2+; optional*).  Follow up workshops run over one or two academic terms.  Participants choose theoretical and/or applied streams.
:::

<br>

::: callout-DSS
##### Advanced
Domain‑specific training (*year 2–3+; tailored*).  Short intensive modules (1-2 days) are scheduled throughout the year based on demand and availability of instructors.
:::

<br>

::: callout-MDS
##### Intermediate
 Methods for Data Science (*year 2+; optional*).  Follow up workshops run over one or two academic terms.  Participants choose theoretical and/or applied streams.
:::

<br>

::: callout-DSS
##### Advanced
Domain‑specific training (*year 2–3+; tailored*).  Short intensive modules (1-2 days) are scheduled throughout the year based on demand and availability of instructors.
:::

:::

::: {.item}
#### Delivery
#### Delivery

Initial delivery is targeted to BBSRC DTP students (2025 cohort), but the programme will be opened to other doctoral and early‑career researchers in subsequent years.  A continuous improvement process ensures that content is updated in response to participant feedback and advances in digital research methods. 

Sessions are delivered primarily in person to foster discussion and collaboration.  Some lectures and code demonstrations are recorded to support revision and remote participation. Participants work in small cohorts, promoting peer learning and networking.  Cohorts are cross‑disciplinary to encourage exchange of ideas. 

Progression through the tiers ensures that participants build confidence gradually.  Assessment is formative: each tier culminates in a small project or portfolio review rather than a high‑stakes examination.  Feedback is provided by instructors and peers.

Progression through the tiers ensures that participants build confidence gradually.  Assessment is formative: each tier culminates in a small project or portfolio review rather than a high‑stakes examination.  Feedback is provided by instructors and peers.

All materials are released under permissive licences via GitHub, enabling reuse and adaptation by the wider research community.
:::
:::::




### Overview

::::: grid
::: g-col-4
### Foundation
The foundation tier serves as the entry point for all participants and runs over four full‑day sessions. It introduces core digital tools and ethical data stewardship through hands‑on scenarios.
:::
::: g-col-4
### Intermediate
The intermediate tier deepens conceptual and practical skills in data science through theoretical and applied streams that combine lectures, seminars and programming workshops.
:::
::: g-col-4
### Advanced
Advanced modules are short, domain‑specific courses covering topics such as bioinformatics, high‑performance computing and geospatial analysis.
:::
:::::

### Foundation: Essential Digital Skills
::: {.tier-card}

**Overview**

The foundation tier serves as the entry point for all participants and runs over four full‑day, in‑person sessions.  Each day is structured around a combination of mini‑lectures, live coding demonstrations, breakout discussions and scenario‑based exercises.  The goal is to familiarise researchers with the digital ecosystem at the University, introduce them to reproducible research tools and lay the groundwork for ethical data stewardship.  Participants learn by doing: small groups work through realistic research scenarios, develop code together and reflect on how the tools apply to their own projects.

**Key modules**

- **Institutional tools and infrastructure** — introduces high‑performance computing clusters, collaborative storage solutions and cloud platforms available at the University.  Participants gain hands‑on experience in scheduling jobs, managing resources and accessing research data stores.
- **Data governance, policy and ethics** — covers legal frameworks (e.g. GDPR), institutional policies on data sharing, confidentiality and consent, and guidance on ethical decision‑making.  Case studies illustrate the consequences of poor data governance and highlight best practices.
- **FAIR principles for data management** — explains the concepts of findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable data; demonstrates how to create metadata, persistent identifiers and data dictionaries; and outlines strategies for long‑term archiving.
- **Core tools: Git, Quarto, R and Python** — introduces version control with Git and GitHub, literate programming with Quarto, and fundamentals of R and Python.  Participants learn how to create and share notebooks, track changes, and collaborate on code repositories.

Throughout the foundation sessions participants build a digital portfolio containing scripts, notebooks and reports that document their learning and can be adapted for their own research.  Peer feedback sessions encourage reflection and support knowledge transfer.

:::

---

### Intermediate: Methods for Data Science

The intermediate tier delves deeper into the conceptual and practical aspects of data science.  After completing the foundation, participants can select one or more streams that match their research needs and disciplinary background.  Each stream combines lectures, seminars and programming workshops, with opportunities to apply the methods to example data sets and to participants’ own projects.  Weekly assignments and formative feedback help consolidate learning.

::::: grid
::: g-col-4
**Theoretical pathway**

This stream focuses on the mathematical and methodological foundations of data‑intensive research.  It begins with a refresher on probability and statistics, emphasising distributions, estimation and hypothesis testing.  Participants then explore linear models, generalised linear models and the principles of experimental design, including randomisation and blocking.  Later sessions introduce unsupervised and supervised machine learning, covering algorithms such as clustering, principal component analysis, regression and classification.  Throughout, the emphasis is on understanding the assumptions behind each method, diagnosing model fit and interpreting results.
:::

::: g-col-4
**Applied pathway (Python)**

This hands‑on stream uses Python and its scientific ecosystem to implement data analyses.  Participants learn to manipulate structured data using pandas, perform numerical computations with NumPy and SciPy, and create publication‑quality visualisations with matplotlib and seaborn.  Sessions cover data cleaning, transformation and summarisation; file input/output; automation of repetitive tasks; and basic exploratory data analysis.  Later modules introduce data pipelines, unit testing and simple automation using the command line.  Emphasis is placed on writing clean, readable code and on using Jupyter notebooks and Quarto documents for reproducible analyses.
:::

::: g-col-4
**Applied pathway (R)**

The R stream caters to researchers who prefer the R programming environment.  It introduces the tidyverse suite of packages, focusing on `dplyr` for data manipulation, `tidyr` for data reshaping and `readr` for data import.  Participants learn to create compelling visualisations with `ggplot2`, perform basic statistical modelling using `lm` and `glm`, and document their work in Quarto documents.  The stream also covers functional programming with `purrr`, data summarisation with `broom` and techniques for creating reproducible reports.
:::
:::::

Each stream encourages participants to bring their own data sets or research questions to class.  This ensures that the concepts are grounded in realistic applications and provides opportunities for peer discussion and cross‑disciplinary collaboration.

---

### Advanced: Domain‑specific training

Beyond the core programme, DS&DSP offers a portfolio of advanced modules tailored to specific research domains.  These optional sessions are typically delivered in small cohorts and are taught by subject‑matter experts from across the University and partner institutions.  The advanced modules allow participants to consolidate the skills gained in earlier tiers and apply them to specialised problems.

**Bioinformatics and computational biology** — introduces genomic and transcriptomic data analysis.  Topics include quality control of sequencing data, alignment, variant calling and differential expression analysis.  Tools such as Bioconductor, `Biopython` and `Nextflow` are used to implement reproducible bioinformatics pipelines.

**High‑performance computing (HPC)** — provides an introduction to parallel computing, GPU acceleration and batch job scheduling.  Participants learn how to profile code, optimise memory usage and adapt scripts to run on clusters and cloud platforms.

**Geospatial analysis** — covers the processing and visualisation of spatial data.  Modules include coordinate reference systems, spatial joins, raster and vector data processing, and mapping with packages such as `sf`, `raster` and `folium`.

**Other special topics** — the programme regularly updates its advanced offerings to reflect emerging research needs.  Past modules have included text mining, social network analysis, and advanced machine learning techniques.


---



---

## Learning outcomes

By the end of the programme participants will be able to:

- **Plan and execute reproducible analysis pipelines** — design workflows using Git, Quarto and Jupyter; track provenance; and automate routine processing.
- **Apply FAIR principles to manage research data** — ensure data and metadata are findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable; create persistent identifiers and follow appropriate licensing.
- **Implement and interpret common data analysis methods** — select appropriate statistical and machine‑learning techniques; perform exploratory data analysis; and critically interpret outputs.
- **Develop clean, readable code** — write and refactor scripts in R and Python; use functions, modules and documentation; and apply version control.
- **Visualise and communicate results effectively** — produce clear plots, tables and reports tailored to diverse audiences; integrate narrative with quantitative findings.
- **Assess and apply data governance and ethics** — navigate legal and ethical frameworks; apply principles of consent, confidentiality and responsible data sharing.
- **Collaborate using digital tools** — contribute to shared repositories, resolve merge conflicts, review code and provide constructive feedback.
- **Translate digital skills to domain‑specific research** — adapt workflows to field‑specific data types, software and analysis requirements.

---

## Access materials

All teaching materials are openly licensed and hosted in a version‑controlled repository.  Each module includes lecture notes, slide decks, example datasets, scripts and step‑by‑step exercises.  Participants are encouraged to clone or fork the repositories, complete the exercises and contribute improvements via pull requests.  The repositories include continuous integration workflows that test code for reproducibility and correctness.

Use the navigation bar or the links below to explore specific modules:

::: panel-tabset
### Essential Digital Skills
👉 [Explore the EDS programme](essential_digital_skills/index.html) — contains the four foundation sessions, reading lists and example notebooks.

### Methods for Data Science (Python)
👉 [Applied Python](methods_for_data_science/applied_python/index.html) — houses the Python stream, including lecture notebooks, assignments and solutions.

### Methods for Data Science (R)
👉 [Applied R](methods_for_data_science/applied_R/index.html) — contains the R stream materials, with tidyverse tutorials, reproducible reporting templates and exercises.

### Domain‑specific modules
👉 [Specialised modules](#advanced-domain-specific-training) — summarises the available advanced modules and links to their individual repositories.
:::

---

## Participant information

The DS&DSP is designed to be inclusive and supportive:

- **Eligibility** — the programme is open to doctoral researchers funded through the BBSRC Doctoral Training Partnership at the University of Nottingham.  Researchers from other funders and early‑career staff may apply if places are available.
- **Prerequisites** — no prior coding experience is required.  The foundation tier assumes only basic computer literacy, and subsequent tiers build gradually.  Participants with existing experience can work on more advanced exercises.
- **Cost** — the programme is funded by BBSRC DTP.
- **Accessibility** — materials are provided in accessible formats (e.g. HTML, PDF with alt text), and sessions are held in accessible venues.  Please contact the programme team to discuss specific accessibility requirements.
- **Support** — teaching assistants and data champions are available during sessions and via online forums to answer questions and provide feedback.  Peer learning is encouraged through group exercises and cohort projects.
- **Community** — participants join a growing community of digital researchers across the University.  Alumni are invited to contribute to later cohorts as mentors and to co‑develop future modules.

---

## Contact

**Programme lead:** Dr Thomas Giles  
📧 <tom.giles@nottingham.ac.uk>

For general enquiries, registration or accessibility queries please contact the DS&DSP administrative team via email at <dsdsp@nottingham.ac.uk>.  You can also follow programme updates on social media using the links in the page header.  We welcome feedback on the curriculum and suggestions for new modules.
